CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Professor B. P. Culverwell, of Trinity, is a gentle-
‘man with his political eye open; he appears to have
“some insight into affairs, notwithstanding that he is a
 Trinity Professor. As early ag October last he threw
cold water on the opera-bouffe Unionists who were
screeching civil war in the event of Home Rule being

~ established, and he advised his fellow-Unionists' to give
up the Ulster plan argument, as it was merely playing
nto the Nationalists hands, and concentrate on finance.

- It was, from the Unionist point of view, very sound
- political advice. Unfortunately for the Unionists, the
‘Home Rule Bill, taken in the lump, was far too good
for their purposes. ~ We have seen that the “Irish
3 es '’ jumped like a hungry tiger at Dr. Starkie's
with o view to using it for Unionist purposes.

or Culverwell sees that the game is up, and

le is inevitable; so, as & shrewd man, he

at he feels it too much to hope he

can defeat. This is'poor consolaiion to Carson and the
never-never-never-shall-be-Irish crowd. In the course
of the Professor’s letter to the ‘ Irish Times '* he says:

1 beg you to use all your influence in support‘oi
the policy of moving amendments honestly in-
tended to improve the Bill. To do so is no bar to
voting against it afterwards. =~ The mere negative
policy of wrecking the Bill proceeds on the agsump-
tion that the Bill will never become law. Tt is to
stake all on onc throw of the dice. and here the
dice are loaded against the Unionists.

The time has come when Irisk Unionists must
face the facts squarely. And of those facts the
most important is that the English voter is-
naturally a Liberal, and that, having ceased to
trouble himself about Home Rule, he will return
to his first allegiance. ~Be sure of this—Home
Rule will never again become the paramount ques-
tion at a General Election. It has been displaced
by the far greater issues due to the industrial and
social revolution through which we are passing.

It will take more than Professor Culverwcll’s letter
to induce the Carrion Crows and the Carsonites to face
facts—they will go down snarling and biting; but let
them snarl and bite as they may, their ascendancy is
gone.

Professor Culverwell gives them some unpleasant
but really interesting statistics. ~ He has got them
from an almanac where anyone could get them, yet
they are very interesting, and we wonder that no one
else has rooted them out before now. Since 1837 the
Tories have only had a majority twice until the Anfi-
Home Rule movement galvanised thein as Unionists
into victory. The Professor says:

The Home Rule question has been the chief
asceb, perheps the only great asset, of the
Conservative Party sinece the Reform Bill.  The
present generation, which has seen that perby
dominant for many years, can hardly realise how
weak has been its hold on the nation. Twice only,
since 1837, have the Conservatives had a majority
at a General Election except on the Home Rule
issue.  Here is the list of the majorities, taken—
from ‘“ Whitaker’s Almanac™ :—

1837—51 Lib. 1880—124 Lib.
1841—81 Cons. 1886 (Jan.)—166 Lib.
1847—1 Lib. 1886 (Aug.)—128 Un.
1852—18 Lib. 1892—89 Liib.
1857—79 Lib. 1895—152 Un.
1859—438 Lib. 1900—184 Un.
1866—67 Lib. 1906—854 Lib.
1868—128 Lib. 1910—124 Liib.
187446 Cons. 1910 (Dec.)—126 Lib.

So few of our readers ever see ‘‘ Alf Fox,” we
may as well quote the remainder of the Professor’s
letter :— .

From 1841 until the ‘“ No Home Rule” cry of
1886 the Conservatives succeeded once, t.e., in
1874, and with the small majority of 46. In 1895
*‘ No Home Rule”” brought them their largest ma-
jority, 152. In 1900 they nearly maintained the
majority, because a nation engaged in a serious
war does not change leaders. By 1906 “ No Home
Rule’” had lost its efficiency, and the country ex-
pressed its disgust with the party which lived on a

The Professor is not quite correct in stating that
** No Home Rule’’ brought the Conservatives a majority
of 152 in 1895, for that Government was not Conser-
vative but a coalition of Conservatives and Liberal
Unionists. We commend to the attention of the Uni
ists who read the LEADER the statement of Prof:
Culverwell that *“ By 1906 ‘ No Home Rule ’ had lost its
efficiency, and the country expressed its disgust with
the party which lives on a negation by the enormous
majority of 854, and even the revolutionary ogramme
of the present Government did not reduce their majo-

rity below 120.”’

There are many foolish Nationalists probably ivho
think that England has little to do but think of Ireland,
but a great many Irish Unionists to-day appear to
suffer from a like ridiculous notion. What does Eng-
land care for the mean shrieks about the ‘‘ scattered ™

Protestants of the ‘ South and West'’ ?

she care about Carson, or William Ireland the off-
licensed Bung, or the like of them ? Ingland is face
to face with great forces and great questions within
her own shores, and the Thirty Shilling Dirties who
are slandering Ireland over there are of little or no
interest to her. England has other things to trouble
her besides the mean wailings inspired by the Unionist
job hunters. Professor Culverwell’s letter is certainly

cold reading for the Irish Unionists.

Is there any country in the world as abnormal as
Ireland ? Here is a common ov garden bobby about
to leave his ‘‘ beat” in Cork and take up some job in
dirty Dublin Castle, and amongst those sending round
the hat for him are the Lord Mayor of Cork, the ex-
Lord Mayor of Cork, and the Eigh Sheriff. And who,
by the way, is Il. Sheehan ?

Under the heading, * Irish and the Insurance Ac_t,”
a correspondent writes:—* The Insurance Act, I think,
is admitted to be abstruse and complicated, and one
which the Government have taken the extraordinary
step of sending lecturers all over the kingdom to explain
its general bearings. In Wales they have taken the
trouble to have literature printed in the native lan-
guage and officials able to speak the language also
appointed.  In Ireland, where fairly large distiicts are
inhabited by persons whose ordinary speech is Irish,
who think in Irish and to whom English is a difficult
and barely understood foreign language, no such pro-
vision has been made, no literature has been published
in Gaelic, no itinerant insfructors or lecturers have
been sent to these districts, and I believe only two tem-
porary officials altogether have been appointed having
a knowledge of the language. Under these circum-
stances it would be well to encourage all who know the
language, and who have to fill any forms in connection
with the Insurance Act, to fill them entirely in Irish.
Let this be done all over the country, and in this man-
ner compel the appointment of Irish scholars and
recognition of the Irish language. Let the Gaels see
to this.”

Try it on the dog. We have the Screeching Sisters
anxious that the Inglish Parliament should settle a
purely Trish question by putting in a clause that under
Home Rule certain women should have a vote. Another
question that is being agitated is Proportional Repre-
sentation. Surely that also can wait until Ireland
settles down under her own Parliament. Of course it
is just the thing to attract many theorists, joboerats
and Unionists.

We have received from Messrs. Sealy, Bryers and
Walker a pamphlet issued by ‘‘ The Proportional Re-
presentation Society of Ircland.”” = The title of the
pamphlet is certainly long enough. I is:— The Elec-
tion of an Irish House of Commons and Senate. Con-
taining an Electoral Map and Schedule of Constituen-
cies.”” - One amusing thing about this movement to
interfere in-elections under Home Rule is that it is
‘‘nion-party,”’ and that numbers of persons who ave
against Home Rule are in it. Anything like the Suffer
movement that might put a difficulty in the way of
Home Rule would naturally attract anti-Home Rulers.
Comparatively new questions like votes for women and
proportional representation can wait so far as Ireland
is concerned. Until Ireland has power to deal with
such matters as a partially self-governing entity, the
springing of this question at a time when the main
thing is to get Home Rule through, is open to not un-
reasonable suspicion. We never heard of the cham-
pions of proportional représentation when we and others
were attempting to break up the monstrous. electoral
arrangement in the Rathmines Township by which the
Protestants can -practically sweep the whole field,
though numerically they are only about half—we think
the lesser half—of the township.  As soon ag the Inish
are to get anything, all sorts of things are suggested.
With the principle of proportional representation, so far
as we understand it, we have no complaint, but these

but if the suggested change, however theoretically ad-

The cominittee of management of Ring Trish College
are appealing for funds to help to reduce a debt of £900
due for the building, and furnishing of the splendid
new Residential College erected by them in 1909. The
work cost £2,200, and in the short space of three years
they have paid off £1,300. There is at present an
urgent need to extend the residential accommodation,
but until the debt is reduced at least by a few hundred
pounds, the committee cannot undertake the erection
of any additional buildings. If the building debts were
paid off the committee would devote any surplus that
might acerue in the future to assisting the local fisher-
men in purchasing modern appliances for their work
so as to help in securing the permanency of the Irish
speaking population of Ring. They appeal earnestly
to their numerous friends throughout Ireland whose
genercsity has enabled them to erect and equip the
College to help them to clear off the debt. Subserip-
tions may be sent to the Trustees:—Messrs. John
[} nn, Maurice O'Hickey, and Dr. Philip Murphy,
Carrick-on-Suir, or to Very Rev. M. Sheehan, M.A.,
D.Ph., St. Patrick’s College, Maynooth, or Very Rev.
Richard Henebry, D.Ph., University College, Cork.

We spent a very enjoyable few hours on Saturday at
Murs. Clarke’s Feis in the grounds of Frankfort, Black-
rock. The Feis, which is an annual affair—the last was ~
the ninth—is known as the Frankfort Feis, but we hope
we may be excused for calling it Mrs. Clarke’s Feis. It
is a great pity there are not a greal many more ladies
like Mrs. Clarke willing to give such hearty and generous
help to the Trish Language movement. Speeches were
delivered by the President of the Gaelic League, Dr.
Douglas Hyde, and Mr. M. A. Ennis, of the Council of
County Councils, and an interesting item of the day’s
proceedings was the excellent rendering of a song in
Irish by the solicitor to the Dublin Corporation, Mr.
Ignatius Rice. The number of competitors was the
highest on record.

THE LEADER.

) the Anti-Home Rule campaign. Dr. Starkie,
5o far as the report that we read goes, did not say any-
 thing about the study of Irish as a national force and
national necessity. We all can’t be schoolmasters,
and we confess we find it very difficult to be wildly
interested in the sayings of Starkie. ~What is chiefly
imnteresting to us is that his address appears to be a
straw ab which Arnott’s paper grabbed with despera-
tion.  Here is one sentence: ““ Dr. Starkic’s statement
put the Nationalist Party on its trial before the
country.”” = Well, so many of that party have gone
through trials by judge and jury, that trial by Starkie
is not likely to rob any of them of a night’s sleep.
The poor Unionists are short of effective weapons.
Some silly placard. in Wexfor —probably put up by
some of the Carson ‘ rebels '"—was seized upon last
week ; the *“ To-hell-with-the Pope ** line appears to be
rather a flat market; the by-elections have become
boomerangs. Plunkett and the hairy fairies constitute
but a frail raft in a troubled sea, and we are magnani-
mous enough to wish the ““ Irish Times’’ the best of
luck with its forlorn hope—Star

The ““ Irish Times,”” under the * strong’ hand of
its editor, the ‘‘ Saved ’* Mr. Healy, is rather amusing,
though somewhat pitiable these days. It knows the mind
of the Unionist Party, which is something more than the
Unionist Party knows itself. It announces: ‘‘ The
Conservative Party, when it returns to office, is pre-
pared to deal, if not generously, at least justly, with
Irish education.”  ““Alf Fox " knows all about it;
perhaps, one of the Cecils told Mahaffy, when the latter
was brushing the former’s coat, and perhaps Mahaffy
told the ‘‘ Saved ** Healy.

This finance bogey is the last scare-crow left to the

* Anti-Home Rulers. It is true that we have paid a
large number of millions in over-taxation, and that
things have been so mismanaged in the way of running

this country against its will that at the present time
we technically are not paying taxes equal to our ex-

penditure.  We are owed a lot of restitution which
we have no chance of recovering; and over a hundred
years of Unionism has left us with many weaknesses
that are not of our making. But yet when we are face
to face with a proposal to keep our own house with
some little financial compensation on foot of the mess
that has been made of our affairs against our will, we
have nothing but contempt for those who cry out for
a continuance of slavery plus more cake. We certainly
endorse the cry of freedom before finance. Like that
phrase of Mr. T. P. Gill, about furmers being trustees
of the nation, the principle, or cry, of freedom before
finance can be operated upon by quibblers; but it is
sound nevertheless. By all means make the best
financial bargain with England—who has swallowed so
many of our millions the while she was obstructing our
development—but if we want money for education, for
arterial drainage, for industrial development, let us
take Home Rule first and look for the money, or do
without it if we cannot get it; afterwards. Having to
~ make ends meet is a very good discipline; and, though
every person would naturally like to give those dear to
them a good start in life, the fact remains that a large
number of people who commence life against great
odds develop so much grit and character that they out-
* distance those who were born with a silver spoon in
their mouth. We are not at all depressed at the
prospect that we as a nation will have “ to do for our-

selves,” and be cut off from any doles from the
““ sister "' who has robbed us of so many millions.
After all the Irish problem is Ireland for the Irish, and
that problem is in the main to retain our unnatural
emigration.  Ireland is no colony like Canada, that
needs to have puffing exhibitions calling all and sundry
to_settle in it and exploit its resources. Ireland is an
old land, and for our part we think the breed is mixed
enough’ already without importing any more. ~We do
not want to expand on mere profit-making principles,
we want to expand as Irish Ireland, and that expansion
must be fairly slow; we are in no furious hurry. If
we stopped our unnatural emigration and attracted a
number of desirable returned exiles to give us a push
along, we don’t see what more we should want. . The
money that we can provide ourselves under the terms
of the Home Rule Bill—the more the financial terms
are improved the better of course—is good enough for
us. If financially we are in a fairly tight cciner

. BOXING.
JHY do I write athletic articles for the LEADER ?

I indulge. The last article I wrote, however, on Irish
athletics was the parent of a most fruitful controversy.
Long after I had retired from tho ring the com-
batants kept hammering one another, the scene of the

- encounter being meanwhile shifted to Cork. The
metaphor brings me to my subject, a most disreputable
one, I fear, “‘boxing.”” " Never have I trembled more
for my reputation as a moralist. One ean write about
battles, even the most cruel and sanguinary, with
applause, that is, battles fought with swords and pistols.
Even duelling is respectable.  Nor would anyone con-
demn the D.M.P. for beating down Trinity College
prowess with their fists, still less object to a description
of that proceeding. As to stone-throwing, indeed, each
of us taking cover behind a tree (many a time have I
done it), perhaps the real combative pastime of the
native Irish boy, the matter is more doubtful. But
boxing! One thinks of Fr. Bernard Vaughan. For the
boxer and the warrior are related much as the petty
thief and the company promoter. The latter classes are
thought worthy of knighthoods.

Now I don’t box myself; I don’t know how. My
experience of the practice is confined to the usual num-
ber of fights and bouts in a schoolboy past, and my pre-
sent reflections were stirred up by witnessing a series of
boxing bouts carried out by National Universi
dents. They recalled to my mind the o
University College years ago when a distinguished
Jesuit boxed a medical student, seeking to force ad-
mission to a hall—and won. The student called next
day to apologise, stating that, under somewhat special
circumstances, he had mistaken him fo1 another mem-
ber of the Order. On a long, lonely walk to Kings-
bridge after the event—I didn’t stop to sce an R.I.C.
man perform ; he will, I suppose, become N.I.C. in due
season—I filled up the time by an examination of
conscience. That commonly results in an article.

Should one attend boxing competitions; or are they
degrading? Now I see to the full the strength of the
case against boxing, and it is a strong case. Of all
sports, boxing has the greatest number of degraded and
evil persons among its patrons.  Criminals are
especially fond of it. The desire to see other people’s
bodies injured and their blood spilt is a vice in itself, a
craving for a morbid ex ion. It is akin to the
other worse vices. This is what brought the Romans
to their gladiatorial shows, and woulgL bring modern
people equally if there were gladiatorial shows to go
to. It is the same vice that brings men to public
executions, where executions are public. ~And it is
this morbid craving and not any scientific interest
which is the real motive of the greater number of
““ patrons *’ and ‘‘ sports,” the people who go to see
prize fights with diamond pins in their cravats. Blood-
- lust.is as old as the world, and is cver waiting for its
_ opportunity to break out. In Iingland and America
this form of degradation has reached its lowest depths.

theatre and boxing. And they had rather a contempt;

movement, and as we are so anxious to point to co-
operative success in Denmark and other countries in
support of our argument for co-operation here, we must
conclude that results must be judged from a similar
standpoint everywhere. Profits, and increase of money
generally, are the basis on which commercial suc-
cess is estimated the world over. Side issues are
all very good in their way. Increased production, ete.,
in any.country cannot be wholly ascribed to one defi-
nite cause, but even admitting for the moment a tan-
gible value in this direction traceable to co-operation
alone, the standard of success must be measured by
all sensible men on the direct profits the capital in-
vested produces, and in the leading co-operative coun-
tries there is no departure from this rule. Incident-
ally T might add that the improved conditions in Ire-
land in recent years might as logically be wholly
ascribed to the change in the course of the Gulf Stream
or the discovery of bluestone for spraying, not to men-
tion the bonus to the landlords, as to co-operation. The
improvement has been merely coincident with, though
in many respects undoubtedly stimulated by, the in-
troduction of the co-operative system.

A few comparative figures with a brief sketch of the
expansion of the chief co-operative institutions in Great
Britain and Denmark, the countrics nearest to home,
is the test I propose to apply to the Irish movement,
taking contemporary periods for illustration. Of
course the movement in Great Britain was well on its
feet before co-operation was introduced into Ireland in
1895 to any noticeable extent. It mainly consisted up
to 1895 of Retail Distributive Societies with two Whole-
sale Societies, but in addition to having obtained com-
mercial success it had performed much pioneer work,
and had been the means of so favourably altering the
laws regulating co-operative trading that it was com-
paratively easy for Irish societies on the same lines to
succeed early in their career. The wholesale societies
in Great Britain were formed for the purpose of collec-
tive buying for the retail societies, but finding a strong
prejudice amongst wholesale suppliers against their
movement, these societies immediately set up their
own factories and workshops and began to produce and
manufacture for their own requirements—a marked con-
trast to the action of the similar Irish societies when at
times their position was supposed to have been assailed.
The gross turnover of the two wholesale societies in
1899 is given at nineteen millions sterling, and in 1909
at thirty-three millions, twenty-four millions worth of
which was produced by their own factories.  During
that period many new enterprises were entered upon
too numerous to detail in this arficle. The gross co-
operative turnover increased to 132 millions, or by 75
per cent., and the membership by 55 per cent., while
agricultural and distributive co-operation mainly con-
cerned with agricultural interests, as in Treland, in-
creased in societies and membership by about 600 per
cent., though the volume of business in connection with
this section did not increase in proportion to the Irish
or nearly so for the same period, while Ireland in
societies and membership advanced by about 50 per
cent. %

Meantime the profits of the cross-Channel movement

third of which is invested in the wholesale societies
as compared with about the one-thousandth part of the
"« capital of the Irish societies invested in the Irish whole-
sale, showing indeed in a pitiable light the lack of con-
fidence Trish societies have in their wholesale society.

TLet us now turn to Denmark, an agricultural coun-
i 3 S )
try like our own, where co-operation and agriculture

* have attained such a degree of perfection that a three

days' visit, armed with an A.B.C. guide, transforms our
Irish trippers into experts and economists. Here too
profits are the main indication of success and the source
of stability. As we are all too well acquainted already
with the general position of co-operation in Denmark,
I will just briefly sketch the history of the Danish Co-
operative Wholesale Society, which was brought into
existence about the same time—1897—as its Irish con-
temporaries.  The position of this society indicates
precisely the position, extent, and national value of the
movement in Denmark, and the same applies to any
rational country. In 1898 this society manufactured
a substantial portion of the requirements of its affi-
liated societies. Its gross turnover increased from
5,826,500 kroner in 1897 to 46,093,000 kroner, approxi-
mately, in 1910 and its manufacturing output from
198,000 to 4,791,000 kroner in the same period. It
paid from 3% per cent. to 5 per cent. dividend on its
share capital all along, and after devoting substantial
sums to organising, educational and other purposes,
made an average of about 4 per cent. profit each year
on its gross turnover.

As a set off to the position and standing of the Irish
societies it may be argued that they have paid en-
hanced prices for home produce or cut prices when sell-
ing to their own members. I disposed of this point in
a previous article, though it is really too ridiculous to
merit serious attention. The reverse is nearer the true
position, so that having regard to the fact that the
1.A.0.8. was liberally subsidised up to quite recently
and in addition the Department paid the salaries of
skilled instructors for a time in nearly all cases where
societies were established, the only rational conclusion
that can be arrived at, taking into account the general
financial position of the movement, is that there
must be something radically wrong. Co-operative
shares are practically valueless in Ireland, and
already heavy losses have been made in now de-
funct societies and federations. Existing investments
produce no interest and as a going concern the whole
bag of tricks would seem to be worth nothing, more
especially as the competing trader is still holding his
own.

Evidently it would seem that instead of subsidising
an outside organisation to usurp its own functions as
well as to organise co-operative societies to divert the
agricultural trade of the country from the individual
trader to co-operative channels, however desirable
the change might be, the Department should
make a reasonable offer for the interest and good-
will of the gombeen man. Experience shows that he
cannot be starved out no matter how substantial the
subsidy, and as he would be of little use to himself or
his country in any other calling but his own, if he is to
be deprived of his interests he is entitled to be recom-
pensed. The landlord who smoothed the path for the
gombeen man is being exterminated by means of the
bonus, and why should the gombeen man who has a
vested interest in his trade be harrassed without recom-
pense % If he has to go and toil and produce for his

THE IRISH PIPES' AND-IRISH PIPERS.

r HE Piper is the distinetive musician of the Gael,

and no movement for reviving the old spirit of
the Celt can be complete or successful which does not:
revive, where extinet, this truly martial instrument of
our race. In Ireland we had unfortunately allowed the
bagpipes almost to die out, and during the latter part
of the nineteenth century it had ceased to be fashion-
able or common amongst us. A stray fiddler or piper
here and there had survived from former days, but he
was becoming but.a more and more oceasional visitor
in our times, and came to be regarded as a tolerated
survival of a bye-gone time. Gaiety and pleasure, as
associated with minstrelsy, music and song, seemed
almost to have died out in Ireland. The terrible and
sad years following the Famine, from black 47 down to
our own day, the dark, drear, and darkening shadow of
gloom and misery was cast over the land and took all
our gaiety away. Emigration and other social troubles
added to. the influences that were drviving from’the
country its old-time love of minstrelsy, music and song.
Even the poor, peripatetic ballad singer at fairs and
markets was becoming extinct. The wandering piper,
that used to attend all social gatherings, and was always
placed high in hall at wedding and other festive
gatherings, was also fast disappearing from- our
midst. The Gaelic movement, however, has
happily arrested this decline of the piper and among
the many other good things that must be placed
to its credit—and it has much creditable and enduring
good to boast of—is the revival of the once popular
piper. He is now, thanks to the self-reliant spir
fused into the people, a prominent person at e
Gaelic gathering, and that is only as it should be in the
eternal fitness of things.. It is only right and proper
that at every Irish gathering—oireachtas or feis—the
piper and his soul-stirring music should stand out as the
leading feature in the musical programme. In Scotland
no meeting of our-Gaelic brethren is considered com-
plete wherein the piper is not well to the front, and at
every social gathering there he is. I have had the
honour: of recently attending some public balls and
dinners in the Highlands, and I always found the bag-
pipes the chief music of the evening. Dressed in their
truly picturesque Gaelic garb, the pipers discoursed
native Celtic airs, some of them our own transplanted
airs, with a spirit characteristic of their sturdy
nationality. =~ Lately I heard at Dundee the Police
Pipers’ Band play a long programme of the beautiful
Scotch airs and tunes which only a Celt can, I believe,
appreciate.  Fancy the Dublin Metropolitan Police
having a pipers’ band, and one too dressed in kilts,
and playing only Irish airs and not the mongrel music
they do attempt to render. And yet there is no reason
why an Irish city should not encourage Irish music in
the same way that a Scotch city does Seotch music, to

_ its -eternal credit be it said, and which is neither

ashamed or afraid of what it calls * Sassanach” in-
fluence.. Every Irish town should also strive to en-
courage its pipers’ band, and those that have set about
it lately deserve honour for so enthusiastically taking
up the matter, and so well doing their part as befits a
patriotic community such as they are.  Dublin, in
this respect, has done and is doing its patriotic part.
This is essentially a people’s movement, and owing to
that fact it is destined to endure and spread. Were if
a Society fad it would not last a generation, and Society
“in Treland has not yet rid itsolf of its old feeble, foible
.for the foreigner—its languid Jonging for looking across
the Channel for inspiration and encouragement, and its

basely imitation of Saxon glidance—its eternal English
ways and its following of English methods and manners.
Leb Society in Ireland be racy of the soil, however, and
if it- wants outside inspiration let it look across to Scot-
land and follow the lead of our Celtic brethren there.
Then will it be respected and self-respecting, and until
it does so become, will be deservedly contemptible.
But the Irish people can act of themselves in these
matters, can draw inspiration and derive encouragement,
from their fellow-Celts in the Highlands, and surely
they need not be ashamed to do what the sturdy Scoteh
are doing. Nay, let them go a step farther, for surely
their spirit of self-reliant patriotism is as vigerous~and
as virile as the Scotch, and if they do they will make
the pipes truly popular.

The pipes have for centuries been the favourite and
characteristic instruments of the Trish people, and that
at a time when their music was the finest and sweetest
in the world. Then were produced airs which have
been justly immortal, and which will now be found em-
bedded in"the musical literature and follk-song of other
countries.  Unfortunately most of our famous Irish
airs were allowed to die out in our land, and leave not
a memory behind with us. But for the noble efforts of
Bunting and others, we should have lost even the rem-
nants that have happily been preserved to us by those
men. In war and peace, on battlefield and ‘n hall, the
piper was the most prominent and favoured person.
Iven so late in our history as the sixteenth century the
piper was a considerable person in Irish social life, and
we read in Stanihurst that in 1575 things were as that
quaint writer thus describes them: “ The Irish like-
wise; instead of the trumpet, make use of a wooden pipe
of the most ingenious structure, to which is joined a
leather bag, very closely bound with bands. A pipe is
inserted in the side of this skin, through which the
piper, with his swollen neck and puffed up cheeks,
blows in the same manner as we do through a tube.
The skin being thus filled with air, begins to swell, and _
the player presses against it with his arms; tlius a loud
and shrill sound is produced through two wooden pipes
of -different lengths. In addition to these there is a
fourth pipe (the chanter) perforated in different places
having 5 or 6 holes, which the player so regulates by the
dexterity of the fingers, that he can cause a loud or
low sound at pleasure.””  This is a very good descrip-
tion of the instrument. In 1566 a Father William
Good, an English Jesuit, who had a school in Limerick,
said of the Trish of his time: ‘‘They love music mightily,
and of all instruments are particularly taken with
the harp. They use the bagpipes in their instead
of the trumpet.”” And in Vincenzo Galilei’s account
of musie, published in Florence in the sixteenth cen-
tury, we read: ‘“ The bagpipe is much used by the
Trish. To its sound this unconquered, fierce and war-
like people march their armies and encourage each
other to deeds of valour. With it they accompany their
dead to the grave, making such mournful sounds
(caoines’or funeral marches) as to invite, nay force, the
bystanders to weep.’’
These few extracts are sufficient T hope to show the
universality and popularity of the bagpipes in former
days in this country. We also read that in war the
Trish also used to march and fight to its sbirring strains
as did the brave Highlanders, and we have carried down
to us many Jacobite airs commemorative of these times.
When Owen Roe O’Neill was buried at Cavan, a band
of pipers played over his grave, and no Irvish or High-
land chief was ever without his piper. He attended
his feasts, followed the clan into war, and ab his obse-
quies played a dirge over his grave. When the ** Wild
Geese '’ crossed to France after Limerick, they brought
their pipers with them, and at Cremona, in 1702, when
the Trish won the day, a tune was composed for the
occasion, and ever afterwards played. Tis name was
“ How We Beat the Germans at Cremona. ™

There were in Ireland many celebrated pipers, even
so late as the beginning: of the nineteenth century, and
curious to say, many of them were blind. To men-
tion only a few the following were noted performers

on the pipes:—Courtenay, Crampton and Crump,
Paddy Conneely (a Galway man), James Gaudsey (a
Kerry man), Thomas O’Hannigan (a Tippersry man),
Paddy O’Sullivan (0’Connell’s piper), Edmund Keating
Hyland (a Tipperary man), Jeremiah Murphy (a Gal-
way man), William Talbot (a Roscrea man).  Hyland
played for George IV., and got a set of costly pipes from
the King. Even so late as 1859 the Tyrone Fusiliers
revived the Irish pipes in the British army, where the
Highland regiments had and have their pipes, but the
Irish were not spirited enough to keep up the pipers’
band, and, like many other Celtic customs in Ireland,
it was abandoned before the advance of English taste
and fashion, the Irish showing less persistency than the
Scotch. Dr. Grattan Flood, the eminent musician and
musical author, whom Enniscorthy can boast of having
the good fortune of possessing as its organist, is the
author of a book on the Bagpipes which will be found
very interesting, and shows the popularity of the instru-
ment once enjoyed in our midst, and may again we
fondly hope.

Mr. Francis Joseph Bigger, the spirited and stout-
hearted Ulsterman, has done yeoman service in the
cause of the revival of the bagpipes. He has formed
a band in Belfast, and I saw his pipers march through
Brussels and many other Belgian cilies some two years
ago. He has creditably done much to encourage the
starting of pipers’ clubs in many cities and towns of
this country, and he deserves eternal credit and praise
for his enthusiasm and efforts in the good cause.

R. J. KELLY.
